chunk_summary: This section outlines a two-visit algorithm for managing growth faltering in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge, emphasizing mechanism-first evaluation, structured escalation, and targeted lab work-up based on pediatric growth faltering frameworks.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for identifying growth faltering in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Learn the stepwise approach to mechanism-first evaluation and structured escalation in growth faltering management.
Recognize the importance of targeted lab work-up and phenotype-based interventions for persistent growth issues.
key_facts: Growth faltering signals include weight gain <15 g/day in late preterm infants, decline across two major percentile lines, or disproportionate weight gain with stagnant length/head growth.
Visit 1 focuses on corrected age verification, feeding observation, and addressing feeding errors before escalation (e.g., increasing frequency/volume or adding fortifiers).
Reassessment is recommended within 5–7 days for higher-risk infants and weekly until stabilization.
Visit 2 involves minimum work-up: CBC, electrolytes, urea/creatinine, urinalysis, stool studies, and phenotype-based tests like ferritin or phosphorus with ALP.
Referral for feeding/swallowing evaluation is advised for persistent aspiration symptoms; inpatient evaluation is necessary for systemic red flags or significant distress.
reference_quote: Treat any of the following as failure signals: weight gain <15 g/day in a late preterm infant or clear slowing of gain, decline across two major percentile lines (or equivalent z-score drop) in weight-for-age or weight-for-length, or weight gain that becomes disproportionate with stagnant length or head growth. — American Academy of Pediatrics
document_key_topics: Growth faltering criteria and management in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Structured escalation tools and reassessment timelines
Targeted lab work-up and phenotype-based interventions
